title: Writing the MT Procedure: Required Content
narrationScript: Every requirement we have covered in this course eventually funnels into one document, the written procedure. This lesson is about what that document must contain, section by section, so that a qualified technician who has never seen the part can run the test and reach the same result you would. That is the standard to write to. Call it the stranger test. The document opens with a foreword. The scope states exactly what the procedure covers. Which parts or part families, which material and condition, which stage of manufacture or service, and which variant of the method, wet fluorescent on a bench unit or dry powder with a yoke in the field. The reference section lists the governing documents by name, edition, and order of precedence, the design code, the customer specification, the industry practice. When two documents disagree, the foreword has already decided which one wins. The procedure carries a revision level, and it carries approvals, developed and approved at Level Three, and accepted by the customer where the contract requires it. Next comes apparatus. Name the equipment the test depends on. The bench unit or mobile power pack and its current types. The yoke. The coil and its number of turns. The particle system by type and designation, fluorescent or visible, wet or dry, and the carrier. The ultraviolet lamp. The meters that prove the conditions, every one of them inside its calibration cycle. Then the settings. Amperage or ampere turns for each shot with its tolerance, shot duration, demagnetization method and target residual field. A setting without a tolerance is not executable. Write one thousand amperes plus or minus ten percent, not one thousand amperes and hope. Then the product. Describe the part, or better, point to a drawing or sketch, and mark the areas to be tested right on it. Coverage claimed must be coverage defined. If only the journal radii and the keyway of a shaft require examination, the drawing says so, and if the whole surface requires it, the drawing says that too. Include the material, the heat treat condition, and the processing stage at which the test happens. After final machining and before plating is a very different examination from as forged. Test conditions come next, the state the part and the station must be in before current flows. Surface preparation, clean, dry, free of oil, scale, and loose paint, with any allowance for thin nonconductive coatings stated explicitly. Temperature limits for the bath. Predemagnetization if arriving parts carry residual fields. Masking of oil passages and bores that must not collect particles. Contact pad condition for direct contact shots. And the lighting environment, verified ultraviolet intensity and booth darkness for fluorescent work, or the white light minimum for visible work. Then comes the heart of the document, the detailed instructions for application. Write them as numbered, imperative steps, one action per step, in order. Verify the daily system checks are current. Clamp the part with proper pad contact. Shot one, direction, current, duration, with the bath applied while the current flows and cut just before the final pulses, the continuous method. Examine and mark. Shot two, perpendicular, repeat. Define the overlap between zones, the section lengths for coil shots on long parts, the rotation of an offset central conductor between shots. Then demagnetize, verify the residual field, postclean, and protect. Finally, the procedure defines how results are recorded and classified. State the classification convention, commonly that a linear indication is one whose length is at least three times its width, because acceptance criteria treat linear and rounded indications differently. Define how locations are reported, zones, datums, distances. Specify the record media, sketch, photograph, or tape transfer, and the report form itself. Part identity, procedure and revision, equipment, settings, particle batch, light meter readings, findings, disposition, and the technician's name, certification level, and date, with the retention period the quality system requires. Write all of it so the stranger succeeds. If a step lives only in someone's head, it is not part of the process, and one retirement, one transfer, or one busy Friday erases it. The procedure is where your training, the standards, and the customer's requirements all meet the bench. Write it tight, validate it, and then follow it exactly.
imagePrompt: Weathered hands fill out a laminated technique card with a grease pencil beside a stepped steel shaft clamped between the contact heads of a wet horizontal bench unit, a magnetizing coil and shrouded ultraviolet lamp in the background.

sections.heading: The Stranger Test — and the Foreword That Sets It Up
sections.body:
A written MT procedure has one job: a qualified technician who has never seen the part runs the document and reaches the same result you would. Every required section below exists to pass that 'stranger test.' The general procedure covers a family of work; a **technique card** carries the validated, part-specific settings — both follow the same skeleton.

The document opens with the **foreword (scope and reference documents)**:

- **Scope** — exactly what the procedure covers: part families, materials and condition, processing stage (in-process, final, in-service), and the method variant (wet fluorescent bench, dry-powder yoke work, and so on). Just as important, what it does *not* cover.
- **Reference documents** — the governing code, customer specification, and standard practices, each by **name and edition**, with the **order of precedence** stated so conflicts are settled in advance.
- **Control** — revision level and history, Level III development and approval signatures, customer or certifying-body acceptance where contracts require it.

An auditor reads the foreword first. So should you — it tells you whether this is even the right procedure for the job in your hands.

sections.caption: The required skeleton of an MT procedure: foreword and references, apparatus and settings, product and test area, test conditions, detailed application instructions, and recording of results.

sections.heading: Apparatus To Be Used — Including Settings
sections.body:
The **apparatus section** names every piece of equipment the examination depends on, specifically enough that the right hardware ends up on the floor:

- **Magnetizing equipment** — bench unit, mobile power pack, yoke, or prods, with available current types (AC, HWDC, FWDC) and output range; coils by number of turns.
- **Particle system** — type and designation: fluorescent or visible, wet or dry, the carrier (conditioned water or oil), and any batch/certification requirements.
- **Lighting and instrumentation** — UV-A lamp, UV and white-light meters, gaussmeter or field indicator, ammeter — each within its calibration cycle.
- **Verification aids** — QQI shims, pie gauge, ring specimen, settling tubes.

Then the **settings**, each with a tolerance: shot amperage or ampere-turns (for example, 1,000 A ± 10%), shot duration and number of shots, bath concentration window, demagnetization method and target residual field. A setting without a tolerance is not executable — the operator cannot hold it, the verifier cannot check it, and the auditor cannot accept it.

sections.heading: Product: Description, Drawing, and Area To Be Tested
sections.body:
The **product section** pins down what is being examined and exactly where.

- **Identification** — part number or family, with a description or (better) a drawing or sketch the technician can hold next to the part.
- **Material and condition** — alloy and heat-treat state matter: they set permeability and retentivity, govern residual-method suitability, and flag delayed-cracking concerns on welded or hardened parts.
- **Processing stage** — 'after final machining, before plating' is a different examination from 'as forged': the surface differs, the target flaws differ, and plating or shot peen would mask what you must find first.
- **Area to be tested** — marked on the drawing: zones, shaded regions, or named features (journal radii, keyway, spline, weld and heat-affected zone). Coverage claimed must be coverage defined. If only critical zones are required, the drawing says so explicitly; if 100% of the surface is required, it says that.

Ambiguity here is how parts ship with an uninspected fillet — the precise failure origin nobody examined because the procedure never clearly said to.

sections.heading: Test Conditions, Including Preparation for Testing
sections.body:
The **test conditions section** defines the state of the part and the station before the first shot:

- **Surface preparation** — clean, dry, free of oil, grease, scale, loose rust, and loose paint over the test area plus an adjacent margin. Any allowance for thin, tightly adhering nonconductive coatings is stated with its limit, because coatings cost sensitivity and thick ones hide flaws.
- **Pre-demagnetization** — required when incoming parts carry residual fields that would distort patterns or cause arc blow at contacts.
- **Masking** — oil passages, bores, and bearing surfaces that must not collect particles are plugged or masked before the bath flows.
- **Contact provisions** — pad condition and clamping force for direct-contact shots, to prevent arc burns.
- **Environment** — bath temperature limits; verified lighting (UV-A ≥ 1,000 µW/cm² at the surface with ambient ≤ 2 fc for fluorescent work, or ≥ 100 fc white light for visible); current daily system checks (settling test, lifting test, lamp check) confirmed **before** production parts run.

If a condition is not met, the procedure says what happens: stop, correct, and verify — not improvise.

sections.title: Write steps, not essays
sections.body: The application section is written as **numbered, imperative steps — one action per step, in execution order**, each with a verifiable value or outcome: 'Energize shot 1: head shot, HWDC, 1,000 A ± 10%, minimum 0.5 s, bath flowing during the shot.' If a technician must infer, the procedure has a hole. And if a step lives only in someone's head, it is not part of the process — one retirement erases it.

sections.caption: A worked technique card: a 12 × 2 in. stepped shaft gets a circular head shot for longitudinal flaws and a five-turn coil shot for transverse flaws, with parameters and tolerances stated.

sections.heading: Detailed Instructions for Application of the Test
sections.body:
The heart of the document is the **step-by-step application sequence**. A sound skeleton for a wet fluorescent bench examination:

1. Verify daily system checks are current (bath settling test, light readings, equipment checks) and the booth meets ambient limits.
2. Prepare and mask the part per the test-conditions section; clamp with full pad contact.
3. **Shot 1 — circular** (example: head shot, HWDC, stated amperage ± tolerance): apply bath **while current flows**, cutting bath just before the final pulses — the continuous method; minimum shot time (commonly ≥ 0.5 s) and number of shots stated.
4. Examine the covered zone under verified UV before repositioning; mark indications as they are found.
5. **Shot 2 — perpendicular** (example: coil shot at the stated ampere-turns): repeat application and examination for transverse flaws.
6. **Coverage rules** — overlap between zones; long parts magnetized in sections (about 18 in. per coil shot, by common practice); an offset central conductor rotated so shots overlap (each shot covering roughly four conductor diameters of circumference).
7. Demagnetize, verify residual field to the stated limit, postclean, and apply protection.

Every step names its value, its tolerance, and what 'done' looks like.

sections.heading: Recording and Classifying the Results
sections.body:
The final required section defines how findings become a record.

- **Classification convention** — state it: by common convention a **linear** indication has length at least **three times** its width; anything else is **rounded**. Acceptance criteria treat the two differently, so the convention must be written, not assumed. Indications are also recorded as relevant or nonrelevant (with the nonrelevant cause noted) and surface or subsurface by appearance.
- **Location reporting** — zones, datums, and distances on the part sketch, so a reader can put a finger on the spot years later.
- **Record media** — dimensioned sketch, photograph, or tape transfer of the indication, as the procedure allows.
- **The report form** — part identity and quantity, procedure number and revision, equipment, current type and amperage per shot, particle type and batch, light-meter readings, findings with sizes and locations, the evaluation result against the named criteria, disposition, and the technician's **name, certification level, and date**.
- **Retention** — how long records are kept, per the quality system and contract.

The test ends when the record is complete — results that cannot be retrieved, understood, and defended later were never really results.

figures.title: Worked technique card for a stepped shaft

interactive.title: File It Where It Belongs
interactive.intro: A draft MT procedure is being assembled and these statements are scattered across someone's notes. Sort each one into the procedure section where it belongs.

items.label: This procedure applies to finished machined steel shafts prior to plating
items.why: A scope statement — which parts, what condition, and what stage — lives in the foreword.

items.label: Governing documents listed by edition, with order of precedence
items.why: Reference documents and their precedence belong in the foreword so conflicts are settled before the bench.

items.label: Head shot: HWDC, 1,000 A ± 10%, minimum 0.5 s per shot
items.why: Equipment settings with tolerances are the apparatus section's core content.

items.label: Wet fluorescent particles in conditioned water, concentration 0.1–0.4 mL/100 mL
items.why: The particle system and its concentration window are apparatus/settings items.

items.label: Drawing zones A–C shaded to show the surfaces requiring examination
items.why: The area to be tested is defined on the product drawing — coverage claimed must be coverage defined.

items.label: Surfaces clean, dry, free of scale and loose paint; oil passages masked
items.why: Surface preparation and masking are test conditions that must be met before current flows.

items.label: Apply bath during the shot, cutting flow just before the final pulses; examine before repositioning
items.why: Continuous-method timing is a numbered step in the detailed application instructions.

items.label: Linear = length ≥ 3 × width; record location from the datum on the part sketch
items.why: Classification conventions and location reporting belong to the recording-of-results section.

checkQuestions.question: In which section of a written MT procedure does 'coil shot: 7,500 ampere-turns ± 10%, two shots, minimum 0.5 s each' belong?
options: Foreword
Apparatus to be used, including settings
Recording of results
Scope statement
checkQuestions.explanation: Magnetizing values with their tolerances are equipment settings — they live in the apparatus section (and on the part's technique card), not in the scope or the records section.

checkQuestions.question: By common convention, an indication is classified as 'linear' when:
options: It glows brighter than rounded indications
Its length is at least three times its width
It is longer than 1 inch regardless of width
It appears only under DC magnetization
checkQuestions.explanation: The widely used convention is length ≥ 3 × width; the procedure must state the convention because acceptance criteria treat linear and rounded indications differently.

checkQuestions.question: What must the foreword of an MT procedure establish?
options: The technician's pay grade and shift schedule
The scope (parts, condition, stage, method variant) and the reference documents by edition with their order of precedence, plus revision and approval control
Only the amperage tables
The brand of particles preferred by the shop
checkQuestions.explanation: The foreword defines what the procedure covers and which documents govern — by name and edition, with precedence stated — under revision control with Level III (and where required, customer) approval.

checkQuestions.question: In the detailed application instructions for the continuous method, the bath timing must read:
options: Apply the bath after the current stops, while the part is still wet
Apply the bath while the current flows, cutting bath flow just before the final pulses, with a stated minimum shot time (commonly about 0.5 s) and number of shots
Dust particles on before clamping the part
Soak the part in the bath overnight before magnetizing
checkQuestions.explanation: The continuous method forms indications while the field is strongest — bath flowing during magnetization and cut just before the final shots so indications are not washed away; timing and shot count are written as verifiable values.

checkQuestions.question: Why must the area to be tested be marked on the product drawing rather than described loosely?
options: Drawings make the procedure look more official
Because coverage claimed must be coverage defined — an ambiguous area description is how a critical fillet ships uninspected
Marking areas is only needed for radiography
So the drawing can replace the report form
checkQuestions.explanation: The examination only proves what was actually covered; defining the test area on the drawing makes coverage unambiguous, auditable, and repeatable by the next technician.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Procedures Outline §5.1 (foreword: scope, reference documents)
CP-105-2024 MT Procedures Outline §5.3 (apparatus, including settings)
CP-105-2024 MT Procedures Outline §5.4 (product description, drawing, area to be tested)
CP-105-2024 MT Procedures Outline §5.5 (test conditions and preparation)
CP-105-2024 MT Procedures Outline §5.6–5.7 (detailed application instructions; recording and classifying results)
Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 and ASME Section V, Article 7 (written procedure requirements — general practice)
